title: Opioid Use Disorder and Medication-Assisted Treatment in a 34-Year-Old Seeking Recovery: Buprenorphine/Naloxone Induction Timed to Moderate Withdrawal (COWS) to Avoid PRECIPITATED Withdrawal, the Three FDA-Approved Medications for OUD, Take-Home Naloxone and Harm Reduction, Stigma-Free Confidential Care, and Comorbidity Screening
subtitle: OPIOID USE DISORDER (OUD) is a CHRONIC, RELAPSING MEDICAL (brain) DISEASE — NOT a moral failing, a character flaw, or a lack of willpower — and is treated as such: with evidence-based medication plus psychosocial support, delivered NON-JUDGMENTALLY and CONFIDENTIALLY. Language matters: use person-first, non-stigmatizing terms (a 'person with opioid use disorder,' 'medication for OUD/MOUD,' a 'positive' or 'negative' toxicology screen — NOT 'addict,' 'clean,' or 'dirty'), because stigma is a major barrier to care. MEDICATION-ASSISTED TREATMENT (MAT) / MEDICATIONS FOR OUD (MOUD) is the STANDARD OF CARE and is NOT 'replacing one addiction with another' — it reduces overdose DEATH, illicit use, infectious-disease transmission, and crime, and improves retention and function. THREE FDA-APPROVED MEDICATIONS: (1) BUPRENORPHINE — a PARTIAL mu-opioid agonist (often co-formulated with naloxone as buprenorphine/naloxone) with a 'CEILING EFFECT' on respiratory depression (lower overdose risk than full agonists) and high receptor affinity; can be prescribed in office-based/outpatient settings; the naloxone component is poorly absorbed sublingually and deters injection/diversion. CRITICAL INDUCTION RULE: buprenorphine must be started ONLY when the patient is already in MODERATE OPIOID WITHDRAWAL — because, as a high-affinity PARTIAL agonist, it will DISPLACE full agonists still occupying the receptors and PRECIPITATE acute withdrawal if given too soon. Use the CLINICAL OPIATE WITHDRAWAL SCALE (COWS) to objectively assess withdrawal severity and TIME the first dose (commonly when COWS indicates at least moderate withdrawal, e.g., a score around 8-12 with clear objective signs, and after an appropriate interval since the last opioid use — longer for long-acting opioids like methadone); start low and titrate. (2) METHADONE — a FULL mu agonist dispensed only through licensed OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAMS (OTPs) with initially DAILY OBSERVED dosing; highly effective for retention; cautions include QTc prolongation, respiratory depression/overdose risk during induction/titration, and many drug interactions. (3) NALTREXONE — an opioid ANTAGONIST (extended-release monthly IM injection) that BLOCKS opioid effects; the patient must be FULLY OPIOID-FREE first (about 7-10 days, longer after methadone) to AVOID PRECIPITATED WITHDRAWAL; best for motivated, already-abstinent patients; no diversion potential and no respiratory depression. All three are combined with COUNSELING/behavioral therapy and recovery support. TAKE-HOME NALOXONE + HARM REDUCTION (core HEALTH-PROMOTION interventions): PRESCRIBE/DISTRIBUTE naloxone (intranasal or IM) to the patient and TRAIN the patient AND family/friends to recognize an overdose (unresponsiveness, slow/absent breathing, pinpoint pupils) and administer naloxone + call EMS + rescue breathing; counsel 'NEVER USE ALONE.' Additional harm-reduction measures: FENTANYL TEST STRIPS, SYRINGE SERVICE PROGRAMS (sterile equipment to reduce HIV/HCV and skin/soft-tissue infections), safer-use education, and connection to care — meeting the patient 'where they are' without requiring abstinence as a precondition for help. CONFIDENTIALITY: federal regulations (42 CFR Part 2) give substance-use treatment records EXTRA protection; provide private, confidential, respectful care. COMORBIDITY + WHOLE-PERSON CARE: SCREEN and treat/refer for HIV, HEPATITIS B and C (and VACCINATE for hepatitis A/B), bacterial infections (endocarditis, abscess, cellulitis), tuberculosis, STIs; co-occurring MENTAL HEALTH disorders (depression, PTSD, anxiety) and other substance use (alcohol, benzodiazepines — dangerous additive respiratory depression); PREGNANCY (buprenorphine or methadone are continued in pregnancy — opioids are NOT withdrawn, as withdrawal risks the fetus; the neonate is monitored for neonatal opioid withdrawal/NAS); contraception; nutrition; dental; social determinants (housing, legal, employment). RELAPSE is understood as a feature of a chronic relapsing disease (like diabetes/hypertension exacerbations) — NOT a failure or a reason to discharge punitively; it prompts re-engagement and treatment intensification. PSYCHOSOCIAL/RECOVERY support: counseling/CBT/contingency management, PEER recovery support/mutual-help groups, case management, and continuity. NURSING ROLE: provide stigma-free, confidential, person-centered care; understand the three MOUDs and the CRITICAL timing rules (buprenorphine only in moderate withdrawal by COWS to avoid precipitated withdrawal; naltrexone only after full opioid abstinence); assess withdrawal (COWS) and support induction; PRESCRIBE/teach TAKE-HOME NALOXONE and harm-reduction strategies; screen and arrange treatment for comorbid infectious and mental-health conditions and special populations (pregnancy); treat relapse as part of the disease; and connect the patient to counseling, peer support, and continuity of care — preventing overdose death and supporting recovery

patient.name: J. Tran
patient.code: Full code; alert with capacity; presenting voluntarily for treatment; goal = start medication for OUD, prevent overdose death (take-home naloxone), reduce harm, screen/treat comorbidities, and engage in recovery
patient.admitDate: Presents to an office-based addiction-treatment program seeking help for OPIOID USE DISORDER. His opioid use began after a prescription for a back injury several years ago and progressed to non-prescribed and illicit opioid use (including fentanyl-contaminated supply); he has tried to stop on his own without success, has had a prior nonfatal overdose, and is now motivated to start treatment. He reports his last opioid use ~16 hours ago and is developing early withdrawal symptoms. He is to be evaluated for BUPRENORPHINE/NALOXONE induction (timed by COWS), prescribed TAKE-HOME NALOXONE, screened for comorbidities (HIV/HCV, mental health), and connected to counseling/recovery support — all with stigma-free, confidential care.
patient.room: Office-based/outpatient addiction-treatment clinic; non-judgmental, confidential setting (42 CFR Part 2 protections); interdisciplinary team (prescriber + nurse + behavioral health/counselor + peer recovery specialist + social work)

labs.n: PRESENTATION + OUD as a chronic disease + stigma-free care
labs.v: 34 y/o man J. Tran presents VOLUNTARILY to an office-based addiction-treatment program seeking help for OPIOID USE DISORDER (OUD). His use began after a legitimate prescription for a back injury several years ago and progressed to non-prescribed and then illicit opioid use (in a fentanyl-contaminated drug supply); he has attempted to stop on his own without success, has had a PRIOR NONFATAL OVERDOSE, and is now motivated for treatment. He reports his LAST OPIOID USE ~16 HOURS AGO and is developing early withdrawal symptoms. KEY FRAMING: OUD is a CHRONIC, RELAPSING MEDICAL (brain) DISEASE — NOT a moral failing, weakness, or lack of willpower — and must be treated NON-JUDGMENTALLY, CONFIDENTIALLY, and with evidence-based medication plus psychosocial support. LANGUAGE matters and reduces the stigma that drives people from care: use person-first, non-stigmatizing terms ('a person with opioid use disorder,' 'medication for OUD/MOUD,' a 'positive/negative' toxicology screen) and AVOID stigmatizing language ('addict,' 'clean,' 'dirty,' 'drug-seeker'). PMH: OUD; hepatitis C positive (per prior testing); HIV status to confirm; possible depression; no other major medical issues reported. SOCIAL: works intermittently; some housing/financial instability; supportive partner who wants to help; ready to engage in treatment. The plan for this visit: assess withdrawal with the COWS and time a BUPRENORPHINE/NALOXONE induction appropriately; PRESCRIBE TAKE-HOME NALOXONE and teach harm reduction; SCREEN and arrange care for comorbidities (HIV, hepatitis B/C, mental health, other substances); and CONNECT him to counseling and peer recovery support — all in a confidential (42 CFR Part 2-protected), respectful, person-centered manner. The nurse's stance — compassionate, non-judgmental, hopeful — is itself therapeutic and improves engagement and retention.
labs.r: OUD is a chronic, relapsing MEDICAL disease (not a moral failing) treated with medication + psychosocial support, delivered NON-JUDGMENTALLY + CONFIDENTIALLY (use person-first language; avoid 'addict/clean/dirty'). This visit: COWS-timed buprenorphine induction + take-home naloxone + harm reduction + comorbidity screening + recovery linkage. RN role: stigma-free, confidential, person-centered care.

labs.n: The three medications for OUD (MOUD) + how they work
labs.v: MEDICATION-ASSISTED TREATMENT (MAT) / MEDICATIONS FOR OUD (MOUD) is the STANDARD OF CARE (reduces overdose death, illicit use, infection transmission, and improves retention/function) and is combined with counseling/psychosocial support; it is NOT 'substituting one addiction for another.' The THREE FDA-APPROVED medications: (1) BUPRENORPHINE — a PARTIAL mu-opioid agonist, frequently co-formulated with naloxone (buprenorphine/naloxone); its PARTIAL agonism produces a 'CEILING EFFECT' that limits respiratory depression and euphoria (LOWER overdose risk than full agonists), and it has HIGH receptor affinity; it can be prescribed in OFFICE-BASED/outpatient settings (improving access); the naloxone component is poorly absorbed sublingually and is included to DETER injection/diversion (if injected, naloxone precipitates withdrawal). (2) METHADONE — a FULL mu-opioid agonist, dispensed only through licensed OPIOID TREATMENT PROGRAMS (OTPs) with initially DAILY OBSERVED dosing; very effective for retention; key cautions = QTc PROLONGATION (baseline/periodic ECG), RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION/overdose risk during induction and titration ('start low, go slow'), and numerous DRUG INTERACTIONS. (3) NALTREXONE — an opioid ANTAGONIST (preferably the extended-release monthly IM injection) that BLOCKS opioid effects (so opioids 'won't work'); it has NO abuse/diversion potential and NO respiratory depression, but the patient must be FULLY OPIOID-FREE before starting (typically ~7-10 days, longer after methadone) to avoid precipitating withdrawal; it is best suited to motivated, already-abstinent patients. CHOICE among the three is individualized (patient preference, prior response, access/setting, pregnancy, comorbidities, diversion concerns, ability to attend an OTP). For THIS patient (actively using, in early withdrawal, seeking outpatient care), office-based BUPRENORPHINE/NALOXONE is appropriate — with the critical caveat about INDUCTION TIMING (next section). Across all options, medication is paired with behavioral treatment and recovery support, and treatment is typically LONG-TERM (often indefinite) — like managing other chronic diseases.
labs.r: Three MOUDs: BUPRENORPHINE (partial agonist; ceiling effect → lower overdose risk; office-based; +naloxone deters injection), METHADONE (full agonist; OTP-only, daily observed; QTc + overdose-in-titration risk), NALTREXONE (antagonist; monthly IM; must be opioid-FREE first). MAT reduces overdose death + improves retention; not 'replacing addiction.' Paired with counseling; long-term. RN role: understand the three MOUDs + individualized choice.

labs.n: Buprenorphine induction timing — COWS + precipitated withdrawal
labs.v: THE MOST IMPORTANT SAFETY/TIMING POINT for buprenorphine: it must be initiated ONLY when the patient is ALREADY in at least MODERATE OPIOID WITHDRAWAL — NOT while full opioid agonists are still meaningfully occupying the mu receptors. WHY: buprenorphine is a HIGH-AFFINITY PARTIAL agonist; if given too soon, it DISPLACES the full agonists from the receptors and, because it is only a partial agonist, the net receptor activity DROPS abruptly — PRECIPITATING acute, often severe WITHDRAWAL (PRECIPITATED WITHDRAWAL). To time the first dose safely, use the CLINICAL OPIATE WITHDRAWAL SCALE (COWS) — a validated tool scoring objective + subjective withdrawal signs (resting pulse, sweating, restlessness, PUPIL size, bone/joint aches, runny nose/tearing, GI upset, TREMOR, yawning, anxiety/irritability, gooseflesh/piloerection) — and begin induction when COWS indicates at least MODERATE withdrawal (commonly a score in the ~8-12+ range WITH clear OBJECTIVE signs), AND after an adequate interval since the last opioid (longer for LONG-ACTING opioids: e.g., ~12-24 h after short-acting like heroin/oxycodone, longer—often 48-72 h—after methadone, given its long half-life). Start with a LOW dose, observe, and give additional doses to titrate to relief of withdrawal/craving on day 1, then maintain. If PRECIPITATED withdrawal occurs, it is managed supportively (and sometimes paradoxically with additional buprenorphine to re-saturate receptors, per protocol) — but the goal is to PREVENT it by correct timing. (NOTE: this differs from NALTREXONE, where the patient must be FULLY OPIOID-FREE for ~7-10 days before the first dose, or it likewise precipitates withdrawal; and from METHADONE, a full agonist that does not require pre-existing withdrawal but carries overdose risk during titration.) NURSING ROLE in induction: educate the patient that they must come in WITH withdrawal symptoms (and not use right before), assess COWS serially, confirm adequate withdrawal before the first dose, administer/observe the sublingual dose (let it dissolve, don't swallow/eat/drink during), monitor the response (withdrawal should improve, not worsen), titrate per protocol, and counsel against concurrent benzodiazepines/alcohol/sedatives (additive respiratory depression). THIS PATIENT'S COWS rose to a moderate level with clear objective signs, the induction dose was given at that point, and his withdrawal improved WITHOUT precipitated withdrawal.
labs.r: Buprenorphine = high-affinity PARTIAL agonist → if given too early it DISPLACES full agonists and PRECIPITATES withdrawal. Start ONLY in at least MODERATE withdrawal, timed by COWS (objective signs; ~8-12+) and after an adequate interval since last use (longer for long-acting/methadone); start low + titrate to relief; observe SL dose; avoid concurrent sedatives. (Naltrexone needs ~7-10 days opioid-FREE first.) RN role: teach to present in withdrawal, COWS timing, administer/observe/monitor.

labs.v: TAKE-HOME NALOXONE and HARM REDUCTION are core HEALTH-PROMOTION interventions that prevent overdose DEATH and disease — offered to ALL patients with OUD (and others at risk), regardless of whether they are abstinent: (1) PRESCRIBE/DISTRIBUTE NALOXONE (intranasal spray or IM auto-injector/kit) to the patient AND ensure the patient's FAMILY/FRIENDS have it and know how to use it (a bystander usually administers it) — TRAIN them to RECOGNIZE an opioid overdose (UNRESPONSIVENESS, SLOW or ABSENT breathing, PINPOINT pupils, blue lips/skin) and to RESPOND: give naloxone, CALL EMS (911), provide rescue breathing/CPR, place in recovery position, and give a repeat dose if no response in 2-3 minutes (naloxone is shorter-acting than many opioids — especially fentanyl — so re-sedation/repeat dosing and EMS transport are essential); counsel that naloxone may precipitate withdrawal but is life-saving. (2) 'NEVER USE ALONE' — using with someone present (or a hotline) so help can be summoned. (3) FENTANYL TEST STRIPS to detect fentanyl in the drug supply. (4) SYRINGE SERVICE PROGRAMS / sterile injection equipment to reduce HIV, hepatitis C, and skin/soft-tissue infections (and 'do not share' equipment). (5) Safer-use education and low-barrier connection to care. The harm-reduction philosophy MEETS THE PATIENT WHERE THEY ARE — it does NOT require abstinence as a precondition for receiving help, and it keeps people ALIVE and connected long enough to enter/continue treatment. This is squarely a NURSING HEALTH-PROMOTION role: teach naloxone use to patients and families, normalize carrying it, provide non-judgmental risk-reduction education, and link to services. THIS PATIENT was prescribed intranasal naloxone with patient + family training, and received harm-reduction counseling (never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services) plus offers of HIV/hepatitis testing and hepatitis A/B vaccination.
labs.r: Take-home NALOXONE for the patient + family (recognize overdose: unresponsive + slow/absent breathing + pinpoint pupils → give naloxone, call EMS, rescue breaths, repeat dose — it's shorter-acting than the opioid) is offered to ALL OUD patients regardless of abstinence. Harm reduction: never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services, safer-use education — meets the patient where they are, keeps them alive + connected. RN role: naloxone training + harm-reduction teaching (Health Promotion).

labs.n: Comorbidities, special populations, relapse, recovery; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: WHOLE-PERSON CARE: people with OUD have important COMORBIDITIES to SCREEN and treat/refer: INFECTIOUS — HIV, HEPATITIS B and C (treat HCV; VACCINATE for hepatitis A and B), bacterial infections (ENDOCARDITIS, abscess, cellulitis, osteomyelitis), TB, STIs; MENTAL HEALTH — co-occurring depression, PTSD, anxiety (integrated treatment improves outcomes); OTHER SUBSTANCES — especially BENZODIAZEPINES and ALCOHOL (dangerous additive respiratory depression with opioids/MOUD — counsel and address); and SOCIAL DETERMINANTS (housing, employment, legal, transportation, food). SPECIAL POPULATION — PREGNANCY: a pregnant person with OUD should be maintained on BUPRENORPHINE or METHADONE — opioids/MOUD are NOT withdrawn (medically supervised withdrawal risks relapse, overdose, and fetal harm); the newborn is monitored and treated for NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (NOWS/NAS) with non-pharmacologic care first; breastfeeding is generally encouraged on stable MOUD. CONFIDENTIALITY: substance-use treatment records carry EXTRA federal protection (42 CFR Part 2). RELAPSE is understood as a feature of a CHRONIC RELAPSING disease (as with diabetes/HTN/asthma exacerbations) — it is NOT a moral failure and is NOT a reason to discharge the patient punitively; relapse prompts RE-ENGAGEMENT and treatment intensification (and a real overdose risk after a period of abstinence due to lost tolerance — reinforce naloxone). RECOVERY/PSYCHOSOCIAL support: counseling/CBT/contingency management, PEER recovery specialists/mutual-help groups, case management/social work, and CONTINUITY. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): buprenorphine was NOT given too early (it was timed to moderate withdrawal by COWS — avoiding precipitated withdrawal); naltrexone was NOT used while he was still using/not yet abstinent (would precipitate withdrawal); the patient was NOT shamed, judged, or refused care, and non-stigmatizing language was used; take-home naloxone was NOT withheld; concurrent benzodiazepines/alcohol were addressed (additive respiratory depression); and OUD was NOT treated as a moral failing or 'cured' by detox alone (detox without ongoing MOUD has high relapse/overdose rates). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a motivated patient presenting voluntarily; access to office-based buprenorphine; correct COWS-timed induction; take-home naloxone + harm-reduction education; comorbidity screening/treatment (HCV, mental health, vaccination); a supportive partner; linkage to counseling + peer recovery + case management; confidential, stigma-free, person-centered care; and continuity/follow-up. EXPECTED outcome (as here): withdrawal/craving controlled on MOUD without precipitated withdrawal, overdose risk reduced (naloxone + lower-risk medication), comorbidities addressed, and engagement in ongoing recovery — recognizing OUD as a treatable chronic disease. NURSING ROLE: stigma-free confidential care; correct MOUD knowledge + induction timing (COWS/abstinence); take-home naloxone + harm-reduction teaching; comorbidity + special-population screening; non-punitive handling of relapse; and linkage to counseling/peer support/continuity.
labs.r: Screen/treat comorbidities: HIV, hepatitis B/C (treat HCV + vaccinate HAV/HBV), endocarditis/abscess, mental health, other substances (benzos/alcohol = additive resp depression). PREGNANCY: maintain buprenorphine/methadone (don't withdraw); monitor newborn for NOWS/NAS. 42 CFR Part 2 confidentiality. RELAPSE = chronic-disease feature (re-engage, don't punish; high overdose risk after abstinence → naloxone). Recovery: counseling + peer support + continuity. Negatives confirm correct practice. RN role: whole-person, stigma-free, evidence-based care.

nursesNote: Office-based addiction-treatment clinic — 34 y/o man J. Tran presents VOLUNTARILY seeking treatment for OPIOID USE DISORDER (began with prescription opioids after a back injury → progressed to illicit/fentanyl-contaminated use; failed self-quit attempts; PRIOR nonfatal overdose; motivated now). Last opioid use ~16 h ago; developing early withdrawal. FRAMING: OUD = chronic, relapsing MEDICAL/brain disease (NOT a moral failing) → non-judgmental, CONFIDENTIAL (42 CFR Part 2), person-first language ('person with OUD,' 'positive/negative screen'; NOT 'addict/clean/dirty'). PMH: OUD, HCV+, HIV to confirm, possible depression. SOCIAL: intermittent work + housing instability + supportive partner. INTAKE: alert/motivated; HR 96, BP 138/86, RR 16, SpO2 99%; early withdrawal (anxiety, yawning, rhinorrhea, sweating, dilated pupils, GI upset). COWS — assessed serially; objective signs increased to MODERATE → induction TIMED to moderate withdrawal to AVOID PRECIPITATED WITHDRAWAL (buprenorphine = high-affinity PARTIAL agonist; if given too early it displaces full agonists → abrupt drop in receptor activity → precipitated withdrawal). INDUCTION: at moderate COWS, gave first LOW sublingual dose of BUPRENORPHINE/NALOXONE (let dissolve; don't swallow/eat/drink); titrated to relief of withdrawal/craving; withdrawal IMPROVED without precipitated withdrawal; partial-agonist CEILING lowers overdose risk; naloxone component deters injection; counseled NO concurrent benzodiazepines/alcohol/sedatives (additive respiratory depression). MOUD options reviewed (buprenorphine partial agonist/office-based; methadone full agonist/OTP daily-observed/QTc; naltrexone antagonist/monthly IM but must be opioid-FREE ~7-10 days first). TAKE-HOME NALOXONE prescribed/dispensed (intranasal) + trained patient + family to recognize overdose (unresponsive, slow/absent breathing, pinpoint pupils) → give naloxone + call EMS + rescue breaths + repeat dose (naloxone shorter-acting than the opioid, esp. fentanyl); HARM REDUCTION (never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services, safer use); offered HIV + hepatitis B/C testing + hepatitis A/B vaccination; screened mental health + other substances. RECOVERY: linked to counseling/behavioral therapy + PEER recovery support + case management; HCV treatment referral + mental-health follow-up; relapse framed as part of a chronic disease (re-engage, NOT punish; high overdose risk after abstinence → reinforce naloxone); close follow-up; confidential, stigma-free care throughout. CONTRAST (teaching): giving buprenorphine TOO EARLY (before moderate withdrawal) → PRECIPITATED withdrawal; starting naltrexone before full abstinence → precipitated withdrawal — both prevented by COWS-timing/abstinence-confirmation. PREGNANCY note: maintain buprenorphine/methadone (don't withdraw); monitor newborn for NOWS/NAS.
providerOrders: VISIT: office-based addiction treatment; opioid use disorder, here for medication-for-OUD induction + harm reduction + comorbidity screening
DIAGNOSIS: opioid use disorder (chronic relapsing disease); in early opioid withdrawal; HCV positive; rule out HIV; screen for co-occurring mental-health/substance disorders
CODE STATUS: full code; alert with capacity; voluntary; care is confidential (42 CFR Part 2)
STIGMA-FREE, CONFIDENTIAL CARE:
Use person-first, non-stigmatizing language; non-judgmental, respectful, person-centered approach; protect confidentiality (42 CFR Part 2)
WITHDRAWAL ASSESSMENT + BUPRENORPHINE INDUCTION:
Assess withdrawal with the Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) serially; instruct the patient to present WITH withdrawal symptoms (do not use right before)

Initiate buprenorphine/naloxone ONLY when in at least MODERATE withdrawal (adequate COWS + objective signs, and an adequate interval since last opioid — longer for long-acting/methadone) to AVOID precipitated withdrawal
Give a low first sublingual dose (let dissolve; no swallowing/eating/drinking during); observe and titrate to relief of withdrawal/craving; maintain thereafter
Counsel: do NOT combine with benzodiazepines, alcohol, or other sedatives (additive respiratory depression)
MEDICATION OPTIONS (individualize):
Buprenorphine (partial agonist; ceiling effect → lower overdose risk; office-based) — chosen here
Methadone (full agonist; opioid treatment program, daily observed dosing; monitor QTc/ECG; overdose risk during titration)
Naltrexone (antagonist; monthly extended-release IM) — requires FULL opioid abstinence (~7-10 days) first to avoid precipitated withdrawal
TAKE-HOME NALOXONE + HARM REDUCTION (offer to all):

Prescribe/dispense naloxone (intranasal/IM); train the patient AND family to recognize overdose (unresponsive, slow/absent breathing, pinpoint pupils) and to give naloxone, call EMS, perform rescue breathing, and repeat dosing (naloxone is shorter-acting than many opioids/fentanyl)
Counsel never use alone; offer fentanyl test strips and syringe-service-program/safer-use information
COMORBIDITY + SPECIAL-POPULATION SCREENING:
Test/treat or refer: HIV, hepatitis B and C (treat HCV); vaccinate hepatitis A and B; assess for endocarditis/abscess/cellulitis/TB/STIs
Screen and treat/refer co-occurring mental-health disorders (depression, PTSD, anxiety) and other substance use (benzodiazepines, alcohol)
Pregnancy: maintain buprenorphine or methadone (do NOT withdraw opioids); monitor newborn for neonatal opioid withdrawal (NOWS/NAS); support breastfeeding on stable MOUD
PSYCHOSOCIAL/RECOVERY + RELAPSE:
Link to counseling/behavioral therapy (CBT/contingency management), peer recovery support/mutual-help groups, case management/social work; address social determinants (housing/employment/legal)
Treat relapse as part of a chronic relapsing disease — re-engage and intensify treatment (do not discharge punitively); reinforce overdose risk after abstinence and naloxone use
FOLLOW-UP:
Close follow-up for maintenance dosing + monitoring; toxicology as clinically appropriate (non-stigmatizing); coordinate ongoing MOUD + behavioral care + comorbidity treatment + continuity

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this patient starting OUD treatment?
steps.inst: Select ALL considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: OUD is a chronic, relapsing medical disease (not a moral failing) — provide non-judgmental, confidential, person-centered care with non-stigmatizing language
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the stigma-free, disease-model framing drives engagement/retention. RN role: respectful, confidential care.

opts.text: Medication for OUD (buprenorphine, methadone, naltrexone) plus psychosocial support is the standard of care and reduces overdose death
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — MOUD is evidence-based + life-saving. RN role: understand/support MOUD.

opts.text: Buprenorphine must be started only in at least moderate withdrawal (timed by COWS) to avoid precipitated withdrawal, because it is a high-affinity partial agonist
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — induction timing is the key safety point. RN role: COWS-timed induction.

opts.text: Prescribe/teach take-home naloxone (to the patient and family) and provide harm-reduction education (never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — naloxone + harm reduction prevent overdose death. RN role: naloxone training + harm-reduction teaching.

opts.text: Screen for and treat/refer comorbidities — HIV, hepatitis B/C (vaccinate HAV/HBV), endocarditis/abscess, mental health, and other substances (benzodiazepines/alcohol)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — whole-person care addresses common, dangerous comorbidities. RN role: comorbidity screening/treatment.

opts.text: Counsel against combining MOUD with benzodiazepines/alcohol/sedatives (additive respiratory depression), and address special populations (e.g., pregnancy → maintain MOUD, don't withdraw)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sedative interactions + pregnancy management are key safety points. RN role: interaction + special-population counseling.

opts.text: Link to counseling/peer recovery support and treat relapse as part of a chronic disease (re-engage, not punish)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial support + non-punitive relapse handling sustain recovery. RN role: recovery linkage + supportive stance.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Give the first buprenorphine dose immediately on arrival, even though he used opioids a few hours ago and has no withdrawal signs yet, to get ahead of withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Giving buprenorphine before adequate withdrawal (with full agonists still on the receptors) PRECIPITATES acute withdrawal. Wait for at least moderate withdrawal by COWS before the first dose.

opts.text: Tell him detox alone (a few days of withdrawal management) will cure his OUD and that medications just substitute one addiction for another, so he should avoid them
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + harmful. Detox alone has high relapse/overdose rates; MOUD is the evidence-based standard and is NOT 'substituting one addiction for another' — discouraging it increases overdose-death risk.

opts.text: OUD is a chronic relapsing brain disease; MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone/naltrexone) + psychosocial support reduces overdose death and improves retention/function
opts.rat: CORRECT — disease model + MOUD efficacy. Foundation for treatment.

opts.text: Buprenorphine is a high-affinity PARTIAL agonist; given before adequate withdrawal it displaces full agonists and precipitates withdrawal, so it is timed to moderate withdrawal by COWS
opts.rat: CORRECT — the precipitated-withdrawal mechanism + COWS timing. Foundation for safe induction.

opts.text: Naltrexone (antagonist) requires full opioid abstinence (~7-10 days) before starting, or it precipitates withdrawal; methadone (full agonist) is dispensed via opioid treatment programs with overdose risk during titration
opts.rat: CORRECT — naltrexone + methadone principles. Foundation for medication selection/timing.

opts.text: Take-home naloxone and harm reduction (never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services) prevent overdose death/infection and are offered regardless of abstinence
opts.rat: CORRECT — harm-reduction principle. Foundation for the Health-Promotion role.

opts.text: Combining opioids/MOUD with benzodiazepines or alcohol causes dangerous additive respiratory depression; and in pregnancy, MOUD is maintained (opioids are not withdrawn)
opts.rat: CORRECT — sedative-interaction + pregnancy principle. Foundation for safety/special populations.

opts.text: Relapse is a feature of a chronic relapsing disease and prompts re-engagement (not punitive discharge); overdose risk is high after a period of abstinence due to lost tolerance
opts.rat: CORRECT — relapse + lost-tolerance principle. Foundation for non-punitive care + naloxone reinforcement.

opts.text: Buprenorphine should be given as soon as the patient arrives regardless of withdrawal status, because the sooner it is started the better
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Starting before adequate (moderate) withdrawal precipitates withdrawal; timing by COWS is essential.

opts.text: Naloxone should be reserved only for patients who are actively still using and withheld from those engaged in treatment to encourage abstinence
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Take-home naloxone is offered to ALL at-risk patients regardless of abstinence/treatment status — it is life-saving and withholding it increases overdose-death risk.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the priorities for this visit

opts.text: Prevent overdose death — prescribe/teach take-home naloxone (patient + family) and harm-reduction strategies
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Overdose death is the immediate life threat in OUD; naloxone + harm reduction are life-saving regardless of what else happens. RN role: naloxone + harm reduction.

opts.text: Assess withdrawal (COWS) and safely time/support the buprenorphine induction to avoid precipitated withdrawal
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Correct, safe MOUD induction engages the patient in life-saving treatment. RN role: COWS timing + induction support.

opts.text: Provide stigma-free, confidential, person-centered care to build trust and engagement
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The therapeutic, non-judgmental relationship underpins retention. RN role: stigma-free confidential care.

opts.text: Screen/treat comorbidities (HIV, hepatitis B/C + vaccination, mental health, other substances) and address special populations
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Whole-person care addresses dangerous comorbidities. RN role: comorbidity screening/treatment.

opts.text: Link to counseling/peer recovery support + continuity, and handle relapse non-punitively
opts.rat: #5 — Important for sustained recovery. RN role: psychosocial linkage + non-punitive follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Assess withdrawal with COWS and initiate buprenorphine/naloxone only once the patient is in at least moderate withdrawal; start low and titrate to relief
opts.rat: Correct COWS-timed induction avoids precipitated withdrawal. RN role: assess + time + titrate.

opts.text: Prescribe/dispense take-home naloxone and train the patient and family to recognize and reverse an overdose
opts.rat: Life-saving harm-reduction/Health-Promotion intervention. RN role: naloxone training.

opts.text: Provide harm-reduction education (never use alone, fentanyl test strips, syringe services) and stigma-free, confidential care
opts.rat: Reduces overdose/infection and builds trust. RN role: harm reduction + respectful care.

opts.text: Screen/treat or refer for HIV, hepatitis B/C (vaccinate HAV/HBV), mental-health, and other substance use; counsel against concurrent benzodiazepines/alcohol
opts.rat: Whole-person comorbidity care + interaction safety. RN role: comorbidity screening + counseling.

opts.text: Link to counseling/peer recovery support; treat relapse as part of the chronic disease and re-engage rather than discharge punitively
opts.rat: Psychosocial support + non-punitive relapse handling sustain recovery. RN role: recovery linkage + supportive stance.

opts.text: For a pregnant patient with OUD, maintain buprenorphine or methadone (do not withdraw) and monitor the newborn for neonatal opioid withdrawal
opts.rat: Correct pregnancy management. RN role: maintain MOUD in pregnancy + NOWS monitoring.

opts.text: Give buprenorphine immediately on arrival despite recent opioid use and no withdrawal signs, to prevent withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — precipitates withdrawal; wait for at least moderate withdrawal (COWS) before the first dose.

opts.text: Start extended-release naltrexone today while he is still using opioids/in withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — naltrexone requires FULL opioid abstinence (~7-10 days) first, or it precipitates withdrawal.

opts.text: Tell him medications just swap one addiction for another and that willpower/detox alone should fix it
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stigmatizing + false; MOUD is evidence-based and detox alone has high relapse/overdose rates. Discouraging MOUD increases death risk.

opts.text: Withhold naloxone because he is entering treatment, to avoid 'enabling' continued use
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — take-home naloxone is offered to ALL at-risk patients regardless of treatment/abstinence; withholding it risks death (especially given overdose risk after abstinence).

opts.text: Use stigmatizing language ('addict,' 'clean/dirty') and confront him about his 'bad choices' to motivate change
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stigmatizing/confrontational language drives patients from care and worsens outcomes. Use person-first, non-judgmental communication.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish a stigma-free, confidential, trusting rapport and take a non-judgmental history
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. A trusting, non-judgmental relationship is the foundation for all subsequent care/engagement. RN role: respectful, confidential rapport.

opts.text: Assess withdrawal with COWS and time the buprenorphine/naloxone induction to at least moderate withdrawal; administer/observe and titrate
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Safe, correctly-timed MOUD induction engages the patient in life-saving treatment. RN role: COWS-timed induction.

opts.text: Prescribe/teach take-home naloxone (patient + family) and provide harm-reduction education
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Naloxone + harm reduction prevent overdose death regardless of the treatment course. RN role: naloxone + harm reduction.

opts.text: Screen/treat or refer for comorbidities (HIV, hepatitis B/C + vaccination, mental health, other substances) and counsel on interactions/special populations
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Whole-person comorbidity care + safety counseling. RN role: comorbidity screening + counseling.

opts.text: Link to counseling/peer recovery support + case management and arrange close, non-punitive follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing. Psychosocial support + continuity sustain recovery. RN role: recovery linkage + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The nurse built non-judgmental rapport, assessed COWS, and timed the buprenorphine/naloxone induction to moderate withdrawal; the patient's withdrawal improved without precipitated withdrawal
opts.rat: Correct, stigma-free, COWS-timed induction engaged the patient in life-saving treatment. Model handling improving his trajectory.

opts.text: Take-home naloxone was prescribed with patient + family training, and harm-reduction education + HIV/hepatitis testing + hepatitis A/B vaccination were provided
opts.rat: Overdose-death prevention + whole-person harm reduction/comorbidity care. Strong, appropriate handling.

opts.text: The patient is stable on buprenorphine/naloxone with controlled cravings, linked to counseling + peer recovery support + HCV treatment, with close follow-up
opts.rat: Engagement in comprehensive, evidence-based care. Expected favorable trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the first buprenorphine dose was given on arrival before any withdrawal (recent opioid use); the patient developed acute precipitated withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Giving buprenorphine too early precipitated withdrawal. Demonstrates why induction is timed to at least moderate withdrawal by COWS.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — extended-release naltrexone was administered while he was still using opioids; he developed precipitated withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Naltrexone requires full opioid abstinence (~7-10 days) first; giving it while opioids are present precipitates withdrawal. Demonstrates the antagonist timing rule.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the team used stigmatizing language and refused naloxone/MOUD, telling him to use willpower; he disengaged from care and later had a fatal-risk overdose
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stigma + withholding naloxone/MOUD drives patients from care and increases overdose death. Demonstrates why stigma-free, evidence-based, harm-reduction care is essential.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he was co-prescribed a benzodiazepine without counseling and combined it with his medication and alcohol, developing respiratory depression
opts.rat: ⚠️ Additive respiratory depression from opioids/MOUD + benzodiazepines + alcohol. Demonstrates the need to counsel against/avoid this dangerous combination.

opts.text: Months later he has a relapse; the team re-engages him non-punitively, intensifies treatment, and reinforces naloxone (overdose risk is high after a period of abstinence)
opts.rat: Correct, non-punitive handling of relapse as a chronic-disease feature + reinforcing naloxone for the post-abstinence overdose risk. Appropriate, recovery-supporting response.

opts.text: Early in the visit, before the induction dose, his withdrawal is present but essentially unchanged while the nurse builds rapport and serially assesses COWS to time the first dose
opts.rat: Withdrawal is appropriately allowed to reach at least moderate severity (serial COWS) before dosing — an unchanged/rising baseline at this point is expected and necessary to avoid precipitated withdrawal.

opts.text: At follow-up he remains on MOUD with no illicit use, engaged in counseling and peer support, HCV treated, carrying naloxone, and functioning better at work and home
opts.rat: Sustained recovery on comprehensive, evidence-based care. Standard ideal long-term outcome for a treatable chronic disease.
